metadata.summary: Load balancer health check types (TCP, HTTP, gRPC, custom), parameter tuning, endpoint design best practices, and implementation examples in Go, Python/FastAPI, and Node.js.

content.title: Load Balancer Health Checks
content.summary: Health checks allow the load balancer to remove unhealthy backends from rotation automatically. Proper design separates liveness (process alive) from readiness (can serve traffic), uses dedicated endpoints, and checks real dependencies — not just HTTP 200.

why.p: Without health checks, a load balancer forwards traffic to crashed or overloaded backends, causing cascading failures and user-visible errors. The goal is fast detection (unhealthy within seconds) without false positives that cause flapping. Separate liveness and readiness probes let Kubernetes and cloud load balancers make fine-grained decisions: restart a crashed process (liveness) vs. stop sending traffic to an overloaded one (readiness) without killing it.

list.item: Any production load-balanced service — health checks are mandatory, not optional.
Services with external dependencies (database, cache, upstream APIs) that can fail independently.
Kubernetes deployments requiring liveness and readiness probes.
Blue-green or rolling deployments where new instances must pass checks before receiving traffic.
Health check path must not execute expensive operations (full DB query, large file load) — it will add latency to every check interval.
Do not use the same endpoint for liveness and readiness — liveness failing restarts the pod; readiness failing only removes it from rotation.

section.name: check-types
section.p: Four standard health check types, ordered by check depth:

list.item: TCP Connect (L4): Verifies the port accepts connections. Fastest, lowest overhead. Use for services where an open port is sufficient proof of availability.
HTTP/HTTPS (L7): GETs a path and checks the status code. Standard for web applications. Configure expected status codes (e.g. 200).
gRPC: Uses the gRPC health checking protocol (grpc.health.v1.Health/Check). Required for gRPC services; standard HTTP checks cannot parse gRPC binary frames.
Script/Custom: Executes arbitrary logic. Use when dependencies are complex (e.g. check that a message queue consumer is keeping up with lag).

section.name: parameters
section.p: Recommended parameter values for most web services:

list.item: Interval: 10-30 seconds between probes. Lower values detect failures faster but increase probe traffic.
Timeout: 5-10 seconds. Must be strictly less than the interval — a timed-out probe that outlives the next interval causes overlapping probes.
Healthy Threshold: 2-3 consecutive successful probes to mark a backend healthy. Prevents flapping when recovering.
Unhealthy Threshold: 2-3 consecutive failures to mark a backend unhealthy. Allows transient failures (GC pause, startup spike) without removing the backend.
Start Period / Grace Period: 30-60 seconds after container start before unhealthy thresholds apply. Required for slow-starting applications (JVM warm-up, DB migration).

rule.statement: Create a dedicated health endpoint at /health or /healthz that checks all critical dependencies (database, cache, required external services) and returns HTTP 200 when healthy or HTTP 503 when any dependency is unavailable.
rule.rationale: Returning 200 regardless of dependency state causes the load balancer to route traffic to instances that will fail every real request. Checking dependencies surfaces real unavailability.

rule.statement: Implement separate liveness (/healthz) and readiness (/readyz) endpoints in Kubernetes deployments. Liveness MUST NOT check dependencies; readiness MUST check them.
rule.rationale: A liveness probe failure triggers a container restart. If liveness checks the database, a database outage restarts all pods in a loop, turning a partial outage into a full one. Readiness removes the pod from Service endpoints without restarting it.

rule.statement: Set health check timeout strictly below the interval, and set the unhealthy threshold to 2-3 rather than 1 to tolerate transient failures.
rule.rationale: A single failure can be a GC pause, a slow network blip, or a probe scheduling delay. Requiring 2-3 consecutive failures before removing a backend prevents unnecessary flapping that itself causes traffic spikes.

rule.statement: Log all health check failures with structured fields (backend_id, check_type, response_code, duration_ms) and alert when the healthy backend percentage drops below 75%.
rule.rationale: Silent health check failures are invisible until traffic errors surface. Structured logs feed dashboards and alerting; percentage-based alerting catches gradual degradation before it becomes an outage.

section.name: endpoint-examples
section.p: Health endpoint implementations in three languages:

example.title: Go health endpoint with dependency checks
example.note: Returns 503 with JSON body when any dependency fails — allows the load balancer to see the error and the ops team to diagnose which dependency failed.

example.title: Python/FastAPI with separate liveness and readiness
example.note: Liveness (/healthz) never checks dependencies; readiness (/readyz) checks only the minimum required to serve traffic. Parallel dependency checks keep response time low.

example.title: Node.js/Express health endpoint
example.note: Promise.all runs dependency checks in parallel, keeping the health endpoint response under the timeout budget even with multiple dependencies.

checklist.check: Create dedicated /health or /healthz endpoint — do not reuse an existing route.
Check all critical dependencies (DB, cache, required external services) in the health response body.
Return HTTP 503 (not 200) when any dependency is unavailable.
Include version/build info and timestamp in response body.
Set check interval 10-30s, timeout strictly less than interval.
Configure startup grace period (30-60s) for slow-starting services.
Log health check failures with structured fields.
Alert when healthy backend percentage drops below 75%.

antipattern.description: Health endpoint that always returns 200 regardless of dependency state.
antipattern.reason: The load balancer routes traffic to instances that will fail every real request. The outage is hidden until users report errors.

antipattern.description: Liveness probe that checks the database connection.
antipattern.reason: A database outage will cause every pod to fail liveness and restart in a loop, converting a partial outage into a full application restart storm. Liveness must only verify the process is not deadlocked.
